title: How Many Times Array is Rotated

statement.markdown: You are given an array of integers that was initially sorted in ascending order. The array was then rotated an unknown number of times. Your task is to find the number of times the array was rotated. The array can contain duplicate elements. The rotation is performed by shifting all elements to the right and moving the last element to the first position. For example, if the initial array is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] and it is rotated twice, the resulting array would be [4, 5, 1, 2, 3].
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n, which is the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n integers, which are the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a single integer, which is the number of times the array was rotated.
statement.notes: The input array can contain duplicate elements.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array.

examples.explanation: The array was rotated twice to get from the initial sorted array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] to the given array [4, 5, 1, 2, 3].
The array was not rotated, as it is already in the initial sorted order.
The array was rotated three times to get from the initial sorted array [1, 2, 3, 4] to the given array [4, 1, 2, 3].
The array was not rotated, as all elements are the same.

hints.text: the array is initially sorted in ascending order, and then rotated an unknown number of times. think about how you can find the minimum element in the array to determine the number of rotations.

since the array can contain duplicate elements, you can't just find the minimum element by comparing the first element with the rest of the array. instead, you need to find the actual minimum element in the array.
once you find the minimum element, you can determine the number of rotations by finding the index of the minimum element in the array.
to find the index of the minimum element, you can use a modified binary search algorithm. start by finding the middle element of the array, and then compare it with the elements before and after it.

if the middle element is greater than the element before it, then the minimum element must be in the left half of the array. otherwise, it must be in the right half. use this logic to narrow down the search space until you find the minimum element.

editorial.title: Finding the Number of Rotations in an Array
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by finding the index of the minimum element in the array, which represents the number of times the array has been rotated. This is because the minimum element will be at the beginning of the unrotated array, and its index in the rotated array will be equal to the number of rotations.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking every possible rotation of the array and counting the number of rotations until we find a match with the given array. However, this approach is slow and inefficient, with a time complexity of O(n^2), making it impractical for large arrays.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach is to use a modified binary search algorithm to find the minimum element in the array, which would reduce the time complexity to O(log n). However, the given solution uses a simple and straightforward approach of finding the index of the minimum element, which has a time complexity of O(n).
editorial.optimalSolution: The given solution is already optimal in terms of simplicity and readability, with a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n) due to the input array. The solution works by finding the index of the minimum element in the array using the index() method, which returns the index of the first occurrence of the minimum element.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that the index of the minimum element in the array is equal to the number of rotations. This is because the minimum element will be at the beginning of the unrotated array, and its index in the rotated array will be equal to the number of rotations.
editorial.dryRun: To test the solution, we can create a sample array and rotate it a certain number of times, then use the solution to find the number of rotations and verify that it matches the expected result.
commonMistakes: Not handling edge cases, such as an empty array or an array with duplicate elements.
Using an inefficient algorithm, such as a brute force approach, which can lead to a high time complexity.
Not considering the case where the array is not rotated at all, in which case the index of the minimum element will be 0.

solutions.python:
import sys
it = iter(sys.stdin.read().split())
n = int(next(it))
nums = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
print((nums.index(min(nums))) % n)
